1: 'In the fourth year of King Darius, the word of YHWH came to Zechariah on the fourth day of the ninth month, which is Chislev.' The date: 7 December 518 BCE — two years after the night visions (which were in the second year of Darius, 519 BCE). The question from Bethel (Zechariah 7:2-3) prompts four oracles in Zechariah 7-8 about fasting and justice. The precise dating grounds the oracle in historical reality and connects it to the ongoing rebuilding project.
2: 'Now Bethel had sent Sharezer and Regem-melech and their men to entreat the favor of YHWH and to ask the priests of the house of YHWH of hosts and the prophets, Should I weep and abstain in the fifth month, as I have done for so many years?' The delegation from Bethel — one of the towns north of Jerusalem — sends representatives to ask about the fasting observance. The fifth month fast commemorated the destruction of the temple (2 Kings 25:8-9; Jeremiah 52:12-13). Now that the temple is being rebuilt, should the fast continue?
3: 'Then the word of YHWH of hosts came to me: Say to all the people of the land and the priests, When you fasted and mourned in the fifth month and in the seventh month, for these seventy years, was it for me that you fasted?' The rhetorical counter-question exposes the true character of the fasting: who benefited from it? The fasts of the fifth month (temple destruction) and seventh month (Gedaliah's assassination — Jeremiah 41:1-3; 2 Kings 25:25) had become ritualized observances, but YHWH asks whether they were genuine expressions of divine mourning or merely human habit and social performance.
4: 'Was it for me that you fasted?' The question is not answered — its force is in its asking. A fast that is truly for YHWH expresses genuine grief over the covenant breach that caused the destruction. A fast that has become a social ritual is for the fasters themselves. The Isaianic critique of fasting (Isaiah 58:1-7) provides the parallel: YHWH's chosen fast is not hunger and sackcloth but releasing the oppressed, feeding the hungry, clothing the naked.
5: 'And when you eat and when you drink, do you not eat for yourselves and drink for yourselves?' The eating and drinking — presumably the feasts that accompanied or followed the fasts — are equally human-centered. The contrast between fasting (for yourself) and eating (for yourself) makes the point: whether abstaining or feasting, the people's religious practice is self-referential. The former prophets had addressed the same audience about the same failure (Zechariah 7:7).
6: 'Were not these the words that YHWH proclaimed by the former prophets, when Jerusalem was inhabited and prosperous, with her cities around her, and the Negeb and the lowland were inhabited?' The oracle links Zechariah to the pre-exilic prophetic tradition: the same message about justice and fasting was preached by Amos, Isaiah, Micah, and Jeremiah. The comfortable pre-exilic Jerusalem (described in the language of agricultural abundance: the Negeb and Shephelah inhabited, cities thriving) heard the same word and did not obey — hence the exile. The post-exilic community faces the same choice.
7: Verse 7 connects the current question to the former prophets' message, covered in Zechariah 7:6's commentary.
8: 'And the word of YHWH came to Zechariah, saying.' A secondary oracle within the chapter — the specific content of what the former prophets demanded is now stated (Zechariah 7:9-10).
9: 'Thus says YHWH of hosts, Render true judgments, show kindness and mercy to one another.' The former prophets' core demand: justice (mishpat emet, true judgment), kindness (chesed), and mercy (rachamim). The triad is the same as Micah 6:8 in different words: justice, covenant loyalty, and compassion. The post-exilic generation being asked the question about fasting is now being reminded that authentic worship requires these social practices, not ritual austerity.
10: 'Do not oppress the widow, the fatherless, the sojourner, or the poor, and let none of you devise evil against another in your heart.' The specific vulnerable groups not to oppress: the four categories of the covenant's marginalized — widow, orphan, sojourner, and poor. These are the same four categories protected by the covenant code (Exodus 22:21-24; Deuteronomy 24:17-22; Zechariah 27:19; Isaiah 1:17; Jeremiah 7:6; Ezekiel 22:7; Malachi 3:5). The inner life clause — 'devise evil against another in your heart' — goes beyond external behavior to intention, anticipating the Sermon on the Mount's internalization of Torah ethics.
11: 'But they refused to pay attention and turned a stubborn shoulder and stopped their ears that they might not hear.' The pre-exilic generation's response to the former prophets' demand: stubborn shoulder (natan kataff soveret, literally 'gave a stiff/stubborn shoulder' — the image of an ox refusing the yoke), stopped ears (hikbidu ozneihem mishmo'a, made their ears heavy against hearing). The animal imagery for human spiritual stubbornness is precise and uncomfortable.
12: 'They made their hearts diamond-hard lest they should hear the law and the words that YHWH of hosts had sent by his Spirit through the former prophets. Therefore great anger came from YHWH of hosts.' Hearts made of diamond (velijivam simu shamir) — the hardest substance known in the ancient world, used to inscribe on rock. The word sent by the Spirit through the former prophets is rejected by stone hearts. The result: great wrath (qetzef gadol) — the exile.
13: 'As I called, and they would not hear, so they called, and I would not hear, says YHWH of hosts.' The reciprocity principle of covenant: as they refused to hear YHWH's word, YHWH refuses to hear their prayers. The same principle in Proverbs 1:24-28 (wisdom's call refused → wisdom laughs at disaster), Isaiah 1:15 ('when you spread out your hands, I will hide my eyes from you'), and Micah 3:4. The divine silence during the exile was the mirror of Israel's human silence during the prophets' ministry.
14: 'And I scattered them with a whirlwind among all the nations that they had not known. Thus the land was desolate, so that no one went to and fro, and the pleasant land was made desolate.' The scattering among unknown nations (hagoyim asher lo yeda'um) — the alienness of the exile reinforces its punitive character: Babylon was not merely distant but unknown, culturally and linguistically foreign. The pleasant land (eretz chemda) made desolate: the land itself was empty and in mourning during the exile, fulfilling the Sabbath-rest of Leviticus 26:34-35 ('then the land shall enjoy its Sabbaths as long as it lies desolate').
